title: German rye bread
subtitle: A dense, moist loaf of whole rye flour
excerpt: A dense German-style loaf made predominantly from whole rye flour. The crumb is tight and moist, almost glossy in texture, with a pronounced earthy flavour and a thick, chewy crust. It keeps for up to a week and slices better on the second day.
sourceNotes: Dense rye loaves (Roggenbrot, Schwarzbrot) are a staple of German baking. A sourdough culture is traditional, but a slow-fermented yeasted version gives similar results. Sources: historic German bread recipes in public-domain household texts; the general structure follows German home-baking convention as documented in nineteenth-century domestic-economy guides.

recipe.freezeNotes: Slice thin and freeze flat. Defrost at room temperature; the dense crumb holds up well.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dough benefits from a slow overnight proof in the fridge after shaping. Bake from cold the next morning.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; drop to 180°C after 20 minutes for a further 40 minutes
baking.steamMethod: water tray on the oven floor for the first 10 minutes

content.text: German rye bread is a dense, moist loaf made mostly from whole rye flour, baked low and slow in a tin. The crumb is almost sticky and pulls clean from a sharp knife; the flavour is earthy and slightly sour, and the loaf improves over the first two days as the starch settles. It is not a bread for sandwiches so much as for thin slices with cheese or cured meat.
Rye flour contains very little gluten-forming protein. The dough is a batter, not a kneadable mass, and should be poured into the tin rather than shaped by hand. This is normal.

items.prepNote: adds colour and a faint sweetness

content.text: Combine the rye flour, yeast, and salt in a large bowl. Add the lukewarm water and treacle, then stir vigorously with a wooden spoon for 2 minutes until fully combined. The mixture is thick and sticky, not a conventional dough. Add the caraway seeds if using. An 80% hydration rye batter should drop heavily from the spoon.
Cover the bowl and leave at room temperature for 2 hours. The batter will not double dramatically; look for small bubbles across the surface and a slight doming in the centre.

Grease a 900 g loaf tin generously and dust with rye flour or seeds. Pour the batter in and smooth the top with a wet spatula. Cover and prove for 4 hours at room temperature, or overnight in the fridge. The batter should rise to just below the rim.

Place a small tray of water on the oven floor. Heat to 220°C fan and bake the loaf for 20 minutes, then remove the water tray and drop the heat to 180°C. Continue baking for a further 40 minutes. The loaf is done when it sounds hollow when tapped and an internal probe reads 98°C. Cool in the tin for 20 minutes, then turn out onto a wire rack. Leave for at least 4 hours before slicing; the crumb firms significantly as it cools.
